exercises.title: A: Gap Fill (High-Speed Rail Text)
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Europe's first high-speed rail service (1) ...... launched in France in 1981, when the TGV began operating between Paris and Lyon at speeds previously unimaginable on rail.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is passive past, so the correct answer is "was".

wrongOptions.1: The bare infinitive (1) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time — watch irregular past forms.

items.sentence: The train runs (2) ...... electricity supplied by a dedicated high-voltage network and is capable of reaching speeds in excess of three hundred kilometres per hour.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is powered by, so the correct answer is "on".

wrongOptions.2: The bare infinitive (2) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (2) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: On-board comfort (3) ...... maintained throughout the journey by an advanced climate-control system, which automatically adjusts the temperature in (4) ...... carriage.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is passive + every carriage, so the correct answer is "is, every".

wrongOptions.3: The bare infinitive (3) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: When the train passes through a tunnel at speed, the pressure-management system compensates automatically, (5) ...... this way preventing any discomfort to passengers.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is fixed phrase, so the correct answer is "in".

wrongOptions.5: The bare infinitive (5) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (5) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: If the train detects an obstruction on the line ahead, the emergency braking system activates (6) ...... to bring the service to a controlled stop.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is reflexive, so the correct answer is "itself".

wrongOptions.6: The bare infinitive (6) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (6) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: Once you are (7) ...... the reservation platform, each leg of your journey (8) ...... be updated or rebooked with a single click.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is on platform + modal, so the correct answer is "on, can".

wrongOptions.7: The bare infinitive (7) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence: (9) ...... are reported to be a number of ambitious expansion projects currently under development across Southern and Eastern Europe.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is existential, so the correct answer is "There".

wrongOptions.9: The bare infinitive (9) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (9) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: Several governments and transport authorities consider (10) ...... to be the most important investment in sustainable infrastructure of the coming decade.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is dummy object, so the correct answer is "it".

wrongOptions.10: The bare infinitive (10) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

exercises.title: B: Word Formation
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: The veteran jazz musician disappeared from the public eye for nearly a decade before staging a remarkable ...... (COME) with a sell-out world tour.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from COME, so the correct answer is "comeback".

wrongOptions.come: The root COME cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "comeback".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of COME that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: COME → comeback: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "comeback" is the word-formation answer from the root COME in this context.

items.sentence: The hiker was visibly exhausted and walking rather ...... (STEADY) along the cliff-top path by the time the rescue team reached him.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is negative adjective, so the correct answer is "unsteady".

wrongOptions.steady: The root STEADY cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "unsteady".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of STEADY that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: STEADY → unsteady: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "unsteady" is the word-formation answer from the root STEADY in this context.

items.sentence: The tribute paid to the retiring headteacher at the end-of-year assembly was one of the most ...... (MOVE) speeches I have ever been privileged to hear.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is moving tribute, so the correct answer is "moving".

wrongOptions.move: The root MOVE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "moving".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of MOVE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: MOVE → moving: Use the -ing form for an activity or gerund.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "moving" is the word-formation answer from the root MOVE in this context.

items.sentence: At the far end of the lane, a narrow cobbled ...... (PASS) led through the old city wall and into the cathedral quarter beyond.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from PASS, so the correct answer is "passage".

wrongOptions.pass: The root PASS cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "passage".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of PASS that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: PASS → passage: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "passage" is the word-formation answer from the root PASS in this context.

items.sentence: The committee has proposed a package of ...... (PROGRESS) reforms designed to improve working conditions and reduce the gender pay gap.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adjective from PROGRESS, so the correct answer is "progressive".

wrongOptions.progress: The root PROGRESS cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "progressive".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of PROGRESS that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: PROGRESS → progressive: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "progressive" is the word-formation answer from the root PROGRESS in this context.

items.sentence: Years of chronic mismanagement and political upheaval had thoroughly ...... (STABLE) the entire regional economy.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is opposite of STABLE, so the correct answer is "destabilised".

wrongOptions.stable: The root STABLE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "destabilised".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of STABLE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: STABLE → destabilised: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "destabilised" is the word-formation answer from the root STABLE in this context.

items.sentence: The team went on to win the championship, the injuries to their three key players ...... (STAND) .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is despite injuries, so the correct answer is "notwithstanding".

wrongOptions.stand: The root STAND cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "notwithstanding".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of STAND that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: STAND → notwithstanding: Use the -ing form for an activity or gerund.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "notwithstanding" is the word-formation answer from the root STAND in this context.

items.sentence: The motorist was caught by a roadside camera and issued with her second ...... (SPEED) penalty of the year.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from SPEED, so the correct answer is "speeding".

wrongOptions.speed: The root SPEED cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "speeding".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of SPEED that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: SPEED → speeding: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "speeding" is the word-formation answer from the root SPEED in this context.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Multiple Choice (Circle the correct word)
exercises.title: C: Multiple Choice (Circle the correct word)

items.sentenceAfter: the new intern a quick induction tour of the building this afternoon?
items.originalSentence: Would you be able to **make / run / do** the new intern a quick induction tour of the building this afternoon?
items.completedSentence: Would you be able to run the new intern a quick induction tour of the building this afternoon?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "run" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.make: "make" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("the new intern a quick induction tour of the building t…").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.make: "make" doesn't fit here → "run" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("the new intern a quick induction tour of the building t…").
optionContrast.do: "do" doesn't fit here → "run" does, because Only "run" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: The coach was careful not to
items.sentenceAfter: the players' expectations too soon before such a difficult fixture.
items.originalSentence: The coach was careful not to **lift / raise / boost** the players' expectations too soon before such a difficult fixture.
items.completedSentence: The coach was careful not to raise the players' expectations too soon before such a difficult fixture.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "raise" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "raise" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "lift" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.lift: "lift" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("the players' expectations too soon before such a diffic…").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.lift: "lift" doesn't fit here → "raise" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("the players' expectations too soon before such a diffic…").
optionContrast.boost: "boost" doesn't fit here → "raise" does, because Only "raise" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: Arguing about the font size of the report is really beside the
items.sentenceAfter: — what matters is whether the content is accurate.
items.originalSentence: Arguing about the font size of the report is really beside the **point / subject / theme** — what matters is whether the content is accurate.
items.completedSentence: Arguing about the font size of the report is really beside the point — what matters is whether the content is accurate.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "point" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "point" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "subject" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.subject: "subject" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("— what matters is whether the content is accurate.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.subject: "subject" doesn't fit here → "point" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("— what matters is whether the content is accurate.").
optionContrast.theme: "theme" doesn't fit here → "point" does, because Only "point" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: When the director offered her a leading role in the film, she
items.sentenceAfter: at the chance without a moment's hesitation.
items.originalSentence: When the director offered her a leading role in the film, she **moved / jumped / ran** at the chance without a moment's hesitation.
items.completedSentence: When the director offered her a leading role in the film, she jumped at the chance without a moment's hesitation.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "jumped" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.moved: "moved" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("at the chance without a moment's hesitation.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.moved: "moved" doesn't fit here → "jumped" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("at the chance without a moment's hesitation.").
optionContrast.ran: "ran" doesn't fit here → "jumped" does, because Only "jumped" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: His lecture was thorough and engaging, but I found the theoretical section in the middle rather difficult to
items.originalSentence: His lecture was thorough and engaging, but I found the theoretical section in the middle rather difficult to **track / follow / watch**.
items.completedSentence: His lecture was thorough and engaging, but I found the theoretical section in the middle rather difficult to follow .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "follow" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "follow" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "track" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.track: "track" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.track: "track" doesn't fit here → "follow" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").
optionContrast.watch: "watch" doesn't fit here → "follow" does, because Only "follow" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: The project seemed impossibly complex at first, but by the final week everything had
items.originalSentence: The project seemed impossibly complex at first, but by the final week everything had **fell / went / came** into place.
items.completedSentence: The project seemed impossibly complex at first, but by the final week everything had fell into place.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "fell" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "fell" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "went" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.went: "went" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("into place.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.went: "went" doesn't fit here → "fell" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("into place.").
optionContrast.came: "came" doesn't fit here → "fell" does, because Only "fell" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: We were sitting on the terrace enjoying the evening when the breeze started to
items.sentenceAfter: distinctly cold, so we moved indoors.
items.originalSentence: We were sitting on the terrace enjoying the evening when the breeze started to **go / turn / get** distinctly cold, so we moved indoors.
items.completedSentence: We were sitting on the terrace enjoying the evening when the breeze started to turn distinctly cold, so we moved indoors.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "turn" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "turn" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "go" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.go: "go" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("distinctly cold, so we moved indoors.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.go: "go" doesn't fit here → "turn" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("distinctly cold, so we moved indoors.").
optionContrast.get: "get" doesn't fit here → "turn" does, because Only "turn" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: Millions of people across the country
items.sentenceAfter: without adequate heating during the coldest months, and that is a serious concern.
items.originalSentence: Millions of people across the country **go / get / become** without adequate heating during the coldest months, and that is a serious concern.
items.completedSentence: Millions of people across the country go without adequate heating during the coldest months, and that is a serious concern.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "go" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "go" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "get" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.get: "get" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("without adequate heating during the coldest months, and…").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.get: "get" doesn't fit here → "go" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("without adequate heating during the coldest months, and…").
optionContrast.become: "become" doesn't fit here → "go" does, because Only "go" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

exercises.title: D: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
López slipped the ball to García in the closing stages of the first half. **By**
García ...... López in the closing stages of the first half.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is passive by-agent, so the correct answer is "was given the ball by".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "was given the ball by", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
Insiders say that auditors have identified a number of irregularities since the audit began. **reported**
There ...... a number of irregularities since the audit began.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is reported perfect, so the correct answer is "are reported to have been".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
Try to keep pace with the leading riders throughout the opening stage of the race. **fall**
Try ...... the leading riders throughout the opening stage of the race.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is don't fall behind, so the correct answer is "not to fall behind".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "not to fall behind", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
The police sealed off the area around the abandoned warehouse for safety reasons. **cordoned**
The area around the abandoned warehouse ...... police for safety reasons.
items.answer: has been cordoned off by the
acceptedAnswers: has been cordoned off by the

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is passive cordon, so the correct answer is "has been cordoned off by the".

wrongOptions.been cordoned off by the: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "has been cordoned off by the", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
A local decorator came and repainted our kitchen for us last spring. **had**
We ...... a local decorator last spring.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is causative had, so the correct answer is "had our kitchen repainted by".

wrongOptions.our kitchen repainted by: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "had our kitchen repainted by", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The department head always likes to thank staff personally on significant professional milestones. **point**
The department head always ...... staff personally on significant professional milestones.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is deliberately does, so the correct answer is "makes a point of congratulating".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "makes a point of congratulating", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
After a hesitant opening, the audience were soon cheering at the comedian's routines. **had**
After a hesitant opening, the comedian ...... at their routines.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is causative had, so the correct answer is "soon had the crowd cheering".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "soon had the crowd cheering", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I just need to deal with this email quickly and then I'll be right with you. **way**
I just need to ...... and then I'll be right with you.
items.answer: get this email out of the way
acceptedAnswers: get this email out of the way

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is deal with quickly, so the correct answer is "get this email out of the way".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "get this email out of the way", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
